extra.reasoning:
back_pain appears only in r1, the highest-priority rule (priority 100), whose output is self_care — the LOWEST acuity in the order [self_care < routine < urgent < emergent]. This creates a monotonicity violation.

Counterexample: fever, slurred_speech, confusion, dyspnea, vision_loss, leg_swelling, sweating all true; focal_weakness false.

Case back_pain = FALSE: r1 requires all 8 of {fever, slurred_speech, confusion, dyspnea, vision_loss, leg_swelling, sweating, back_pain}; with back_pain false, r1 does not fire. The remaining matching rules are r2/r3/r8/r9 (priority 2, urgent) and r4/r5/r6 (priority 3, emergent). Highest priority wins: priority-3 rules fire -> acuity = emergent.

Case back_pain = TRUE (all else unchanged): now all 8 conditions of r1 are satisfied, so r1 fires at priority 100, dominating the priority-2 and priority-3 rules -> acuity = self_care.

Setting back_pain from false to true lowers acuity from emergent to self_care. Since self_care < emergent, the monotonicity property ("setting back_pain true never lowers acuity") is violated. The witness_assignment is the back_pain=true input that yields the lower acuity (self_care).
